description:
You are a security engineer who has been asked to do a very small-scope credential rotation for a legacy service that keeps its secrets in two formats.  
Everything you need is already under /home/user/credentials.

Files that exist right now  
1. /home/user/credentials/settings.yml  
2. /home/user/credentials/settings.toml  

Current (old) values found in BOTH files  
• access_key_id = OLD_ACCESS_KEY  
• secret_access_key = OLD_SECRET_KEY  

New values that must be put in place  
• access_key_id = NEW_ACCESS_KEY_2023  
• secret_access_key = NEW_SECRET_KEY_2023  

What you must do  
1. Edit /home/user/credentials/settings.yml so it ends up **exactly** like this (including spacing and new-lines):

aws:
  access_key_id: NEW_ACCESS_KEY_2023
  secret_access_key: NEW_SECRET_KEY_2023
database:
  user: dbuser
  password: verysecret

2. Edit /home/user/credentials/settings.toml so it ends up **exactly** like this (including quotes and new-lines):

[aws]
access_key_id = "NEW_ACCESS_KEY_2023"
secret_access_key = "NEW_SECRET_KEY_2023"

[database]
user = "dbuser"
password = "verysecret"

3. Create a plain-text log file /home/user/credentials/rotation.log that contains ONE single line (with a trailing newline) in the following fixed format. The automated test will check it verbatim, so match it exactly:

2023-10-05T12:00:00Z|OLD_ACCESS_KEY|NEW_ACCESS_KEY_2023

Important rules the grader will enforce  
• After you finish, the strings OLD_ACCESS_KEY and OLD_SECRET_KEY must not appear anywhere in either settings.yml or settings.toml.  
• rotation.log must contain exactly the one line shown above and nothing else (except its final newline).  
• File paths and file names are case-sensitive; spell them exactly as given.

This is a tiny task—editing two files and writing one log entry—so it should only take you a couple of commands.
truth:
Initial state before the agent starts
-------------------------------------
Directory /home/user/credentials exists.

1. /home/user/credentials/settings.yml (permissions 0644) currently contains:

aws:
  access_key_id: OLD_ACCESS_KEY
  secret_access_key: OLD_SECRET_KEY
database:
  user: dbuser
  password: verysecret

2. /home/user/credentials/settings.toml (permissions 0644) currently contains:

[aws]
access_key_id = "OLD_ACCESS_KEY"
secret_access_key = "OLD_SECRET_KEY"

[database]
user = "dbuser"
password = "verysecret"

There is no /home/user/credentials/rotation.log yet.

Expected final state
--------------------
1. /home/user/credentials/settings.yml (permissions still 0644):

aws:
  access_key_id: NEW_ACCESS_KEY_2023
  secret_access_key: NEW_SECRET_KEY_2023
database:
  user: dbuser
  password: verysecret

2. /home/user/credentials/settings.toml (permissions still 0644):

[aws]
access_key_id = "NEW_ACCESS_KEY_2023"
secret_access_key = "NEW_SECRET_KEY_2023"

[database]
user = "dbuser"
password = "verysecret"

3. /home/user/credentials/rotation.log (permissions 0644, newly created) contains **exactly**:

2023-10-05T12:00:00Z|OLD_ACCESS_KEY|NEW_ACCESS_KEY_2023\n

(no extra blank lines or spaces)

Verification logic
------------------
• Grep both settings files for the literals “OLD_ACCESS_KEY” or “OLD_SECRET_KEY”; none should be found.  
• Both new keys must be present exactly as shown.  
• rotation.log must exist, be non-empty, contain exactly one line, and that line must equal the string specified above (including trailing newline).
